blocks.text: Today's briefing: AI coding agents are starting to ship at non-technical companies, Anthropic's coding lead admits the scaling laws still hold, and a quiet disagreement is opening up about whether junior PMs should be writing production code at all.
Two of today's interviews land on the same fault line. On Lenny's Podcast, Zevi Arnovitz — a non-technical PM at Meta — argued the bar has fundamentally shifted for new graduates entering tech.

blocks.speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
blocks.text: It's the best time to be a junior, contrary to what a lot of people are saying — when else in history could you get out of school and just build a startup on your own?

Cross-cutting against that, Boris Cherny — head of Claude Code at Anthropic — told the AI Daily Brief his team is increasingly worried about the asymmetry: senior engineers are getting compounding leverage, juniors are getting outpaced by the people they used to learn from.

blocks.speaker: Boris Cherny
blocks.text: The scaling laws didn't bend, they just moved — and the people who already knew how to ship are pulling away from the ones who are still learning.

Onto our second story — Q1 capital flows. BG2Pod's Brad Gerstner walked through the Altman call, the Apollo private-credit footprint, and what he calls the “big three” trades for the rest of the year.

blocks.speaker: Brad Gerstner
blocks.text: Private credit is doing the heavy lifting public markets used to do, and the spread is wider than anything we've seen since the GFC.
Also today: Founders Forum on the new EU AI Act enforcement timetable, Acquired's two-part on the Rivian comeback, and Latent Space on the Anthropic–Cursor latency war. Links in the index below.
That's the briefing for May 8th. Back tomorrow.
